questions.question: Distinguish between verification and validation in software engineering, providing an example of each activity.
keyPoints: Verification: checking if the product meets specified requirements (are we building the product right?)
Validation: checking if the product meets user needs and operational context (are we building the right product?)
Example of verification: peer reviews, static analysis, unit testing against design specs
Example of validation: user acceptance testing, system integration testing in real environment

questions.question: Briefly describe the purpose of ISO/IEC 25010 quality model and list two quality characteristics it defines for software products.
keyPoints: ISO/IEC 25010 defines a quality model to evaluate software product quality, replacing ISO/IEC 9126
Two quality characteristics: 'Functional Suitability' and 'Performance Efficiency' (or any other pair)
Provides measurable sub-characteristics (e.g., completeness, accuracy, time behaviour, resource utilization)
Used to define acceptance criteria and to benchmark product quality

questions.question: Discuss the defect management lifecycle, including the key stages, roles involved, and how severity and priority are assigned. Illustrate with a diagram description and mention tools commonly used for tracking defects.
keyPoints: Defect lifecycle stages: New → Assigned → Open → Fixed → Verified → Closed → Reopened (if needed)
Roles: Tester (reports), Developer (fixes), QA Lead (prioritizes), Manager (tracks metrics)
Severity (impact on system) and Priority (urgency of fix) are assigned using matrices, e.g., Severity 1-Critical, Priority 1-High
Tools: JIRA, Bugzilla, Mantis, IBM Rational DOORS; often integrated with CI/CD pipelines

questions.question: Explain three key software test techniques (e.g., equivalence partitioning, boundary value analysis, and decision table testing). For each technique, describe its underlying principle, suitable application scenario, and any associated quality metrics it helps to improve.
keyPoints: Equivalence Partitioning: divide input domain into equivalence classes; reduces test cases while maintaining coverage; improves test efficiency metric
Boundary Value Analysis: test values at edges of partitions; catches off-by-one errors; raises defect detection rate
Decision Table Testing: capture combinations of inputs and actions in a table; useful for complex business logic; increases test coverage of combinatorial paths
These techniques collectively raise code coverage, defect density, and test effectiveness metrics
